transcript.clean_text: Welcome to highlights of stage 8, right here on GCN Racing. Yesterday, we finished up the wall that is the Mas de la Costa, and when you have gradients like that, it’s very hard to beat this man. Alejandro Valverde, at the Vuelta, in the World Champions jersey, taking a hugely popular stage victory. 2nd on the day was Primoz Roglic, but third place was enough to see Miguel Angel Lopez go back into the red jersey, just 6 seconds ahead of Roglic. Today, stage 8 was somewhat less hilly, but by no means a guaranteed sprint finish. The start was in Valls, outside a sports hall named after my former teammate, one of the nicest guys you could ever meet, Xavi Tondo. Rest in peace Xavi. The opening part of the stage was on rolling terrain, and then with a 2nd category climb coming with around 40kms to go, this was going to be a tough one for the fast men. After 14kms, we had 21 riders up the road. The biggest names being Luis Leon Sanchez of Astana, Zdenek Stybar of Deceuninck Quickstep, Sergio Henao of UAE, plus previous stage winners Dylan Teuns of Bahrain Merida and Jesus Herrada of Cofidis. His teammate, Nicolas Edet, was also in there and the best placed on GC, 6 minutes and 24 seconds behind Miguel Angel Lopez. Astana were left with the responsibility of controlling the tempo in the main group behind, but the gap continued to rise, 6 minutes with 46kms remaining. On to the only categorised climb of the day, then, and it was a bit cagey amongst the riders in the breakaway. Edet keen to press on, thinking more about the stage than the GC at this point. The pace behind wasn’t exactly flat out, but it was enough to see a few riders dropped. Amongst them, KOM leader and stage winner Angel Madraso of Burgos BH, along with Fabio Jakobsen, Dutch national champion. At the front by now was Peter Stetina of Trek Segafredo, a good climber, but not a sprinter, this was where he would need to make the difference. And he was doing a fantastic job, clawing out an advantage of almost half a minute by the time he’d got just over half way up the climb. However, he’d soon have company in the form of Fernando Barcelo, the 23 year old from the Euskadi Basque Country Murias team. Towards the top, the weather had started to take a turn for the worse, but the riders weren’t letting that effect their concentration, Jesus Herrada wanted to add another stage victory to his Palmares, and although he wouldn’t quite reach the leading duo by the top of the climb, he’d manage to get onto their wheels down the descent. There was still all to play for, though - there was just 13 seconds separating them from the rest of the group behind. By this point, Nicolas Edet was creeping towards being the virtual race leader on the road - were Astana and Miguel Angel Lopez about to lose the red jersey for the third time in the race? With 25kms to go, it was pretty much all back together at the front, and in fact, some of the stage would be decided on this treachorous descent. Luis Leon Sanchez was amongst a few riders who’d get distanced down it, and he’d play no further part in the outcome of the stage. By this point, Edet WAS in the virtual race lead, but we had four riders off the front. Guerrrero of Katusha Alpecin, Tusveld of Sunweb, Barcelo and Aranburu of Caja Rural, a man who has been on tremendous form recently. Just as it was coming back together, yet again, Tusveld decided to go on the counter attack. And he was looking good, until this. Going slightly too hot into a wet roundabout, he’d hit the deck, his hopes of a stage win over in an instant. The last 10kms was in fact full of attacks, this is Tobias Ludvigsenn of Groupama FDJ on the move, closely followed by Stybar. And in fact it was Stybar who caught Ludvigsen, going straight past him and setting off to try and take his first Vuelta stage win since 2013. Half of the battle in the finale was simply to stay upright, the roads incredibly slippery. Stybar would eventually be caught inside the closing kilometre, setting up a cagey and nervous sprint. Guerrero was the first to kick, but flying straight past him, in the saddle, was NIkias Arndt. Rare to see somebody sprinting like this, but it was clearly affective, nobody was able to get even close to the German, who crossed the line with a good few bike lengths to spare. Brilliant win by Arndt, his 2nd Grand Tour stage win, but interestingly the first time he’s crossed the line first - his previous stage win at the Giro only came about after Nizzolo was disqualified for veering off his line. Jubilation for Team Sunweb, too - they’ve had a very tough season in 2019, but their Vuelta has already been a successful one. The peloton would eventually roll in over 9 minutes down on the race winner, and so, yet again, we had a new race leader, and by quite some margin. Tomorrow, it’s a little beast! Just 94.4kms but with barely a metre of flat around the notoriously tough roads of Andorra. 5 categorised climbs, including the first especial of this year, the Coll de la Gallina. And then a mountain top finish up the Cortals d’Encamp, 2095m above sea level. My prediction, carnage. And Quintana. See you tomorrow.
